title: Common Geoms: Histogram, Boxplot, Bar Chart
description: Create histograms, boxplots, and bar charts with geom_* functions.

slides.title: Histograms with geom_histogram()
slides.content:
Histograms show the distribution of a single continuous variable. You only need to map `x`.

```r
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = mpg)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 10)
```

You can adjust bin width with `binwidth` or number of bins with `bins`.

slides.title: Histogram Example
slides.codeSnippet:
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = mpg)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 2, fill = "skyblue", color = "black")

quizData.question: For a histogram, which aesthetic is required?
quizData.explanation: Histograms only need an x mapping (the continuous variable).

slides.title: Boxplots with geom_boxplot()
slides.content:
Boxplots visualize the distribution through quartiles. Often you map a categorical variable to x and continuous to y.

```r
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = factor(cyl), y = mpg)) +
  geom_boxplot()
```

slides.title: Boxplot Example
slides.codeSnippet:
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = factor(cyl), y = mpg, fill = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_boxplot()

slides.title: Bar Charts with geom_bar()
slides.content:
Bar charts are for categorical data. By default, `geom_bar()` counts the number of cases at each x position.

```r
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_bar()
```

If you want the heights to represent values in a column, use `geom_col()` or `geom_bar(stat = "identity")`.
